programs.name: Utah Medicaid Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical services for low-income seniors and individuals with disabilities, including long-term care options like nursing homes and in-home care.[2][5] It covers health care costs for eligible beneficiaries once approved.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 65+ and people with disabilities meeting income and asset requirements.[4][5]
programs.benefit_value: Pays for medical services, nursing home care, and waiver-based in-home services; value varies by need and eligibility.

programs.name: Utah PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings, including adult day health care and transportation. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid-covered care to support aging in place.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 55+ who are nursing facility eligible, live in PACE service areas, and can safely reside in the community.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage for all needed services; average annual value exceeds $50,000 per participant.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Helps pay Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors.[5] Administered through Utah Medicaid as prescription drug and premium assistance.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries meeting specific income/asset limits (e.g., QMB up to 100% FPL, SLMB 120%, QI 135%).[5]
programs.benefit_value: Premium payments ($170+/month for Part B), deductibles, and cost-sharing; saves up to $5,000+ annually.

programs.name: Utah SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card to purchase food for low-income households.[5] Helps ensure nutritional needs are met for seniors on fixed incomes.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors (60+) and disabled individuals meeting income guidelines.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per household; max for one person ~$291/month (2026 rates).

programs.name: Utah LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Offers help with heating/cooling bills, crisis assistance, and weather-related repairs for low-income households. Administered statewide for energy needs.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+ meeting income eligibility (up to 150% FPL).
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants $200-1,000+ for bills; crisis aid up to $600.

programs.name: Utah Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency in low-income homes through insulation, sealing, and heating upgrades. Reduces utility costs and health risks from poor conditions.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households (200% FPL), prioritizing seniors, disabled, and families with children.
programs.benefit_value: Free home upgrades worth $5,000-10,000, saving $300+/year on energy.

programs.name: Utah SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free counseling on Medicare options, appeals, and extra help programs.[5] Helps seniors navigate coverage and save money.
programs.who_its_for: All Utah Medicare beneficiaries, especially low-income seniors.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one advice; helps access thousands in savings annually.

programs.name: Utah Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Often includes safety checks and friendly visits.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ who are unable to prepare meals, with suggested donations.

programs.name: Utah Caregiver Support Program (UCSP)
programs.what_it_does: Supports family caregivers with respite, counseling, training, and access to services to delay institutionalization.[4] Includes no low-income requirement for core services.
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid family caregivers of adults 60+, those with Alzheimer's/related disorders, or older relatives (55+) caring for children/disabled.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Respite up to 240 hours/year, counseling, and supplies; value $2,000-5,000/year.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides community service work training and paid part-time jobs to unemployed low-income seniors.[4] Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income adults 55+ unable to find work.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage part-time work (20 hrs/week), ~$7,000/year income/support.

programs.name: Utah Legal Services for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Offers free legal aid for issues like housing, benefits, abuse, and consumer problems. Partners with Area Agencies on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing civil legal needs.
programs.benefit_value: Free representation; saves thousands in legal fees.

programs.name: Utah Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, resolving complaints and protecting rights. Provides education and investigation services.
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities in Utah.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy and complaint resolution; improves quality of life and care.

programs.name: Utah Aging Waiver
programs.what_it_does: Provides long-term home and community-based services (HCBS) for elderly state residents who require nursing home level of care, aiming to prevent unnecessary institutionalization. Services include in-home personal care, adult companion services, respite care, home modifications, assistive technology, and personal emergency response systems.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Utah residents aged 65 or older requiring nursing home level of care, determined by functional and financial eligibility; not an entitlement with limited slots and waitlist.[1]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific 1915(c) Medicaid HCBS waiver tailored for seniors 65+, offering a broad array of non-medical supportive services like chore services (snow removal, lawn care) and adult companion services not universally available in other states' waivers.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by needs; covers services up to nursing home level without fixed cash amount; limited enrollment slots.[1]
